text: Not all social media mob dynamics are morally equivalent. Sociologist Ruha Benjamin distinguishes between digital pile-ons that target the powerful — executives, institutions, policy makers — and those that target private individuals or marginalized groups. The former can function as distributed accountability in the absence of formal institutional recourse; the latter amplify existing power asymmetries and cause disproportionate harm. Treating all collective online pressure as equivalent 'mob behavior' performs its own political work: it discredits legitimate forms of distributed public accountability by associating them with harassment. The mechanism is identical; the ethics are not.
question: What political function does the passage claim is performed by treating all collective online pressure as equivalent 'mob behavior'?

options.text: It protects private individuals from disproportionate scrutiny
It discredits legitimate distributed accountability by associating it with harassment
It equalizes the power dynamics between institutions and individuals
It highlights the shared mechanism underlying all forms of collective pressure
